title: Listening - Part 3: Doing Voluntary Work
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about their reasons for doing voluntary work. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the reason each speaker gives. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about their reasons for doing voluntary work. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the reason each speaker gives. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about why they began doing voluntary work.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: Two years ago my dog escaped during a storm. People from the local animal rescue centre spent an evening searching for him and eventually brought him home safely. I never forgot that. [19]When I had some free time this year, volunteering at the centre seemed the obvious way to repay the help my family had received.[/19] Cleaning cages isn't glamorous, but every time an animal is reunited with its owner, I understand why they do it.||Speaker 2 Man: There's a stream near our estate where I used to play as a child. Over the last few years it became full of plastic bottles and rubbish, and people stopped walking there. I complained once, but that achieved nothing. [20]I joined the weekend volunteer group because I wanted to do something practical about a neglected place in my own area.[/20] We've cleared most of the banks now, and families are coming back again.||Speaker 3 Woman: My nephew is football-mad, but his junior team nearly had to close when their coach moved away. His parents are busy at weekends, and he was terribly disappointed. I'd played a little football years ago, although I'm no expert. [21]I offered to help run the training sessions mainly because I couldn't stand the idea of my nephew losing the activity he loved.[/21] I enjoy it more than I expected, especially when the quieter children score.||Speaker 4 Man: I used to avoid speaking to strangers, so serving drinks at our community theatre sounded like the last role I should attempt. My sister encouraged me to try one evening. [22]I kept returning because each performance made it a little easier to welcome visitors and start conversations without worrying about saying the wrong thing.[/22] Now I even introduce the shows occasionally. I still get nervous, but I no longer let that stop me.||Speaker 5 Woman: When I retired, I suddenly had whole days with no colleagues around. My children live some distance away, and I found the quiet difficult. The library needed volunteers for its weekly reading café, which sounded pleasant. [23]I signed up largely because I hoped to meet people regularly and become part of a group again.[/23] That has certainly happened: the other volunteers and several of the visitors now feel like old friends.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: Two years ago my dog escaped during a storm. People from the local animal rescue centre spent an evening searching for him and eventually brought him home safely. I never forgot that. When I had some free time this year, volunteering at the centre seemed the obvious way to repay the help my family had received. Cleaning cages isn't glamorous, but every time an animal is reunited with its owner, I understand why they do it.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: There's a stream near our estate where I used to play as a child. Over the last few years it became full of plastic bottles and rubbish, and people stopped walking there. I complained once, but that achieved nothing. I joined the weekend volunteer group because I wanted to do something practical about a neglected place in my own area. We've cleared most of the banks now, and families are coming back again.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: My nephew is football-mad, but his junior team nearly had to close when their coach moved away. His parents are busy at weekends, and he was terribly disappointed. I'd played a little football years ago, although I'm no expert. I offered to help run the training sessions mainly because I couldn't stand the idea of my nephew losing the activity he loved. I enjoy it more than I expected, especially when the quieter children score.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: I used to avoid speaking to strangers, so serving drinks at our community theatre sounded like the last role I should attempt. My sister encouraged me to try one evening. I kept returning because each performance made it a little easier to welcome visitors and start conversations without worrying about saying the wrong thing. Now I even introduce the shows occasionally. I still get nervous, but I no longer let that stop me.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: When I retired, I suddenly had whole days with no colleagues around. My children live some distance away, and I found the quiet difficult. The library needed volunteers for its weekly reading café, which sounded pleasant. I signed up largely because I hoped to meet people regularly and become part of a group again. That has certainly happened: the other volunteers and several of the visitors now feel like old friends.

options.text: to deal with a local problem
to act as a positive role model
to make friends and feel they belong

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker volunteers at a rescue centre to repay the help it gave her family (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker joined a group to tackle rubbish in a local natural area (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker volunteered as a coach chiefly so his nephew could continue playing football (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker continued volunteering because it developed her confidence in dealing with people (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker volunteered in order to meet people and recover a sense of belonging (evidence [23]).
